authoritative_sources.title: Google Developer Documentation Style Guide
authoritative_sources.source_class: Client-delivery, documentation, or engagement workflow guidance
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: Google publishes this official developer documentation style guide for software documentation clarity, terminology, examples, cross-references, accessibility-aware writing, and developer-audience conventions.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: Documentation intake, drafting, terminology control, and quality review

authoritative_sources.title: Microsoft Writing Style Guide
authoritative_sources.source_class: Client-delivery, documentation, or engagement workflow guidance
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: Microsoft publishes this official style guide for clear, concise technical content, terminology, inclusive language, and user-task-oriented documentation.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: Documentation drafting, maintainability review, localization readiness, and editorial quality checks

authoritative_sources.title: Writing for GitHub Docs
authoritative_sources.source_class: Client-delivery, documentation, or engagement workflow guidance
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: GitHub publishes this documentation contribution guidance for its own docs workflow, including content structure, style, reusable content, and quality expectations relevant to software documentation review.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: Documentation repository workflow, review preparation, and publication-readiness handling

authoritative_sources.title: About Releases
authoritative_sources.source_class: Client-delivery, documentation, or engagement workflow guidance
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: GitHub publishes official release guidance for versioned software release artifacts, release notes, binaries, and links between tags and release records.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: Release note, migration note, versioning, and publication handoff

authoritative_sources.title: CommonMark Specification 0.31.2
authoritative_sources.source_class: Professional standards or recognized public frameworks
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: CommonMark is the published specification for a strongly defined Markdown syntax baseline, making it authoritative for Markdown-first documentation artifact compatibility.

authoritative_sources.title: OpenAPI Specification
authoritative_sources.source_class: Professional standards or recognized public frameworks
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: The OpenAPI Initiative publishes the official OpenAPI Specification for HTTP API descriptions used to generate and validate API reference documentation.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: API reference evidence validation and contract traceability

authoritative_sources.title: RFC 2119: Key words for use in RFCs to Indicate Requirement Levels
authoritative_sources.source_class: Professional standards or recognized public frameworks
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: The RFC Editor publishes this IETF requirements-language reference for normative terms such as MUST, SHOULD, and MAY, which constrains technical documentation acceptance criteria and API behavior claims.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: Acceptance criteria, API behavior wording, and normative requirement review

authoritative_sources.title: Web Content Accessibility Guidelines (WCAG) 2.2
authoritative_sources.source_class: Professional standards or recognized public frameworks
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: W3C publishes WCAG 2.2 as a recognized web accessibility standard that informs accessible documentation structure, link text, headings, alternatives, and content usability.

authoritative_sources.title: Secure Software Development Framework (SSDF) Version 1.1
authoritative_sources.publisher: National Institute of Standards and Technology
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: NIST publishes SSDF as federal secure software development guidance with common vocabulary for software producers and consumers, supporting evidence-backed security and quality documentation expectations.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: Security documentation evidence mapping and secure-development claim review

authoritative_sources.title: Application Security Verification Standard
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: OWASP publishes ASVS as a recognized application security verification framework, making it authoritative for mapping security-related documentation claims to verification evidence without granting certification.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: Security documentation requirement and verification evidence review

authoritative_sources.title: SP 800-171 Rev. 3, Protecting Controlled Unclassified Information in Nonfederal Systems and Organizations
authoritative_sources.publisher: National Institute of Standards and Technology
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: NIST publishes SP 800-171 Rev. 3 for protecting CUI in nonfederal systems and organizations, providing an authoritative public confidentiality baseline for restricted documentation inputs and outputs.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: Confidential source handling, restricted documentation review, and publication blocking

authoritative_sources.title: Records Management Language for Contracts
authoritative_sources.publisher: U.S. National Archives and Records Administration
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: NARA is the U.S. federal records authority and publishes records-management language that defines record ownership, maintenance, disposition, and contractor handling concepts useful for documentation recordkeeping boundaries.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: Record maintenance, retention, handoff, and disposition boundary

workflow_stages.name: Record and document intake
workflow_stages.description: Capture the documentation request, intended audience, product area, release target, source record IDs, approval state, confidentiality classification, and tenant documentation policy before drafting or reviewing.
source_classes: Client-delivery, documentation, or engagement workflow guidance

workflow_stages.name: Evidence and source-of-truth validation
workflow_stages.description: Validate feature briefs, design notes, acceptance criteria, code diffs, API contracts, schema files, test results, architecture records, runbook context, release records, style policy, and reviewer assignments before making documentation claims.
source_classes: Professional standards or recognized public frameworks
Client-delivery, documentation, or engagement workflow guidance

workflow_stages.name: Documentation planning, drafting, and maintenance recommendation
workflow_stages.description: Prepare Markdown-first documentation plans, edits, acceptance criteria, release notes, migration notes, runbooks, and review reports with explicit source traceability, assumptions, version target, and publication or retention disposition.
source_classes: Client-delivery, documentation, or engagement workflow guidance
Professional standards or recognized public frameworks

workflow_stages.name: Review, approval, confidentiality, and publication readiness
workflow_stages.description: Check source support, format, links, terminology, accessibility, localization, security claims, confidentiality, approval owner, and publication readiness without claiming final editorial, legal, security, compliance, contractual, release, or attestation authority.
source_classes: Professional standards or recognized public frameworks
Client-delivery, documentation, or engagement workflow guidance

workflow_stages.name: Retention, handoff, and exception routing
workflow_stages.description: Produce record update notes, evidence packets, audit-trail summaries, exception notes, and adjacent-owner routing when records are missing, conflicting, confidential, unapproved, stale, or outside the technical documentation lane.
source_classes: Client-delivery, documentation, or engagement workflow guidance

artifacts: feature briefs, product requirements, user stories, design notes, acceptance criteria, and implementation tickets
code diffs, pull requests, API contracts, OpenAPI files, schemas, interface examples, dependency notes, and test results
architecture diagrams, decision records, runbook context, incident summaries, observability notes, and support workflow records
release plans, changelogs, migration constraints, compatibility notes, rollback expectations, and support readiness records
documentation repository pages, Markdown files, knowledge-base articles, link inventories, glossaries, reusable snippets, and publication branches
review comments, approval matrices, confidentiality classification, retention policy, publication checklist, exception logs, and adjacent-owner handoff notes
Markdown-first documentation plans and edits, structured review reports, evidence packets, record update notes, audit-trail summaries, and escalation notes
systems_of_record: tenant documentation repository or knowledge base
source code repository and pull request system
issue tracker, product requirements system, or engineering planning board
test result, CI evidence, and release artifact repository
runbook, incident, and support knowledge repository
tenant policy library for style, confidentiality, accessibility, localization, approval, publication, and retention rules

allowed: classify a request as in-scope, blocked, out-of-scope, or requiring orchestrator return
prepare source-backed documentation plans, documentation edits, review reports, release notes, migration notes, runbooks, record update notes, evidence packets, and exception summaries
recommend documentation acceptance criteria, terminology, structure, source citations, evidence gaps, review owners, approval dependencies, and publication-readiness conditions
map documentation claims to approved engineering, API, architecture, test, release, support, style, confidentiality, and tenant-policy records
route work to adjacent specialists or human reviewers when authority shifts
escalate: required feature, API, design, test, release, approval, confidentiality, or tenant-policy records are missing, stale, or contradictory

documentation claims depend on unresolved engineering behavior, security controls, legal interpretation, contractual commitments, client rights, professional judgment, editorial signoff, release approval, or attestation authority
public standards and tenant policies conflict on terminology, accessibility, confidentiality, retention, review order, or publication authority
systems of record disagree about source-of-truth status, implementation behavior, API contract, release target, owner approval, or retention disposition
the request requires implementation coding, infrastructure execution, release pipeline ownership, production operations, legal or compliance certification, or product strategy
refuse: cloud platform operations, IAM administration, networking, patch deployment, production troubleshooting execution, or infrastructure configuration requests
application code implementation, database migration, CI/CD change, release pipeline execution, or rollback execution requests
legal opinion, licensing negotiation, compliance certification, privacy impact determination, contractual approval, warranty language, or attestation signoff requests
final release approval, final publication approval, security certification, editorial signoff, or contractual commitment without explicit tenant authority
record publication, deletion, reclassification, external sharing, or long-term retention outside approved systems and permissions
generic business strategy, pricing, GTM, or marketing claims that are not technical documentation evidence

domain_failure_modes: Treating technical documentation as generic copywriting and omitting engineering source records, version target, API contracts, tests, approval state, or retention handoff.
Producing confident documentation despite missing feature briefs, code diffs, API contracts, schemas, test evidence, reviewer approval, or tenant style and confidentiality policy.
Normalizing conflicting records instead of escalating source-of-truth disagreements between design notes, API contracts, tests, runbooks, release records, or tenant policy.
Publishing or recommending unrestricted sharing of confidential customer, incident, security, architecture, credential, or tenant material without authorization.
Overstating authority by claiming final release, editorial, legal, security, accessibility, compliance, contractual, or attestation approval.
Drifting into coding, infrastructure operations, release engineering, incident response, product strategy, licensing, legal advice, or compliance certification.
Using public standards as a substitute for tenant-specific policy, approval matrices, publication workflows, or reviewer assignments.
Allowing stale or unreviewed source baselines to drive documentation claims after the refresh interval or when source decay is flagged.

scenario_family_coverage.scenario_family: Client request that crosses into advisory or signoff authority
Confidentiality or approval conflict in delivery workflow
Escalation for missing authority or conflicting records
Client or content workflow handling with evidence and approval awareness
Escalation when confidentiality, professional judgment, or contractual authority changes the action
Normal in-scope execution using realistic industry terminology and artifact shapes
Boundary rejection when the request should move to an adjacent specialist
Low-confidence handling when required facts, records, or authority sources are missing
Conflicting-source or conflicting-policy handling that requires escalation instead of confident synthesis
Tenant-specific adaptation handling that preserves the public baseline and records assumptions

unresolved_ambiguities.topic: Tenant-specific documentation authority and publication workflow
unresolved_ambiguities.detail: Public documentation and recordkeeping sources establish baseline workflow and quality expectations, but each tenant must supply documentation repository permissions, reviewer assignments, publication gates, retention rules, and confidential content handling. The package treats those facts as runtime prerequisites and escalates when absent.

unresolved_ambiguities.topic: Product-specific engineering truth and source-of-record precedence
unresolved_ambiguities.detail: Public API, Markdown, security, style, accessibility, and release sources cannot determine product-specific behavior. The package requires tenant feature briefs, code diffs, API contracts, tests, architecture records, and release approvals before making product claims.

unresolved_ambiguities.topic: Jurisdictional legal, privacy, accessibility, and contractual requirements
unresolved_ambiguities.detail: The public baseline supports safe documentation practice, but legal, privacy, accessibility compliance, contractual, and attestation conclusions vary by tenant, jurisdiction, and engagement. The specialist records caveats and routes to the proper owner instead of deciding them.

notes: No unresolved ambiguity is blocking for this validated package because missing tenant authority, source-of-record precedence, confidentiality, legal, contractual, privacy, accessibility, and approval gaps are explicit runtime prerequisites and escalation triggers.

The lane stops before implementation coding, platform operations, release engineering execution, incident response, product strategy, legal advice, license negotiation, compliance certification, final editorial approval, final publication approval, or attestation authority.

Research output constrains the final spec pack by requiring source-backed claims, named systems of record, explicit review and approval state, confidentiality-aware workflow, and scenario coverage for missing evidence, authority boundaries, and conflicting records.
